questions.question:
The sum of n terms of the series 4 + 44 + 444 + ... is:

A. (4/81)(10^(n+1) - 9n - 10)
B. (4/81)(10^n - 9n - 1)
C. (4/81)(10^(n+1) - 9n - 10)
D. (4/81)(10^n - 9n - 16)
questions.to_find: Sum of n terms of the series 4 + 44 + 444 + ...

understand.plain: The series 4 + 44 + 444 + ... has each term formed by repeating the digit 4. We can write each term as 4 * (111...1 with k digits) = (4/9) * (10^k - 1). Summing n such terms gives a closed-form expression involving a geometric sum of powers of 10.

keywords.explain: Each term of the series is 4 followed by k fours, which equals (4/9)(10^k - 1).
The sum of 10 + 10^2 + ... + 10^n = 10(10^n - 1)/9 is a standard geometric series.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; purely algebraic manipulation of a series.

given.aim: Find a closed-form formula for S_n = 4 + 44 + 444 + ... (n terms)

terms.connects: Allows us to write each term using powers of 10
Used to sum the 10^k parts of each term

terms.meaning: sum_{k=1}^{n} k = n(n+1)/2, but here we just need sum_{k=1}^{n} 1 = n
terms.example: The constant -1 part from each term contributes -n total
terms.connects: Used to handle the constant part of (4/9)(10^k - 1)

given.plan: Write T_k = (4/9)(10^k - 1), sum from k=1 to n, separate geometric and constant sums, simplify.

steps.title: Express k-th term
steps.note: Factor out 4/9 from the repunit structure.

steps.title: Sum all n terms
steps.note: Split the sum into a geometric part and a constant part.

steps.title: Evaluate the geometric sum
steps.note: Standard geometric series with ratio 10 and first term 10.

steps.title: Simplify to final form
steps.note: Combine numerators over common denominator 9 inside the bracket.

questions.formula_note: The geometric series formula sum r^k = r(r^n-1)/(r-1) is the key tool here.

questions.question:
How many integers are there between 100 and 1000 all of whose digits are even?

A. 60
B. 80
C. 100
D. 90
questions.to_find: Count of integers between 100 and 1000 with all digits even

understand.plain: We need 3-digit numbers (100 to 999) where every digit is even. Even digits are 0, 2, 4, 6, 8 — five choices. The hundreds digit cannot be 0 (otherwise it would not be a 3-digit number), so it has 4 valid choices. The tens and units digits each have 5 valid choices.

keywords.explain: The set of even single digits: {0, 2, 4, 6, 8}, giving 5 possible values.
keywords.example: The digit 4 is even; 3 is not even.

keywords.explain: The hundreds digit of a 3-digit number cannot be 0, so it must be 2, 4, 6, or 8 — only 4 choices.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 420 160" width="420" height="160"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="420" height="160" rx="12" class="bg"/><rect x="20" y="30" width="110" height="100" rx="8" class="hi"/><rect x="155" y="30" width="110" height="100" rx="8" class="bg"/><rect x="290" y="30" width="110" height="100" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="75" y="22" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">Hundreds</text><text x="210" y="22" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">Tens</text><text x="345" y="22" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">Units</text><text x="75" y="88" text-anchor="middle" class="big ink">4</text><text x="210" y="88" text-anchor="middle" class="big ink">5</text><text x="345" y="88" text-anchor="middle" class="big ink">5</text><text x="75" y="118" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">{2,4,6,8}</text><text x="210" y="118" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">{0,2,4,6,8}</text><text x="345" y="118" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">{0,2,4,6,8}</text><text x="145" y="88" text-anchor="middle" class="big ink">x</text><text x="280" y="88" text-anchor="middle" class="big ink">x</text><text x="210" y="148" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ok">Total = 4 x 5 x 5 = 100</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three boxes showing digit position choices: hundreds has 4 choices (non-zero even), tens and units each have 5 choices (any even digit).

given.aim: Count 3-digit integers (100-999) where all digits are even

terms.meaning: Integers strictly between 100 and 1000, meaning 3-digit numbers: 100 to 999.
terms.example: 248 is in range; 46 is not (2 digits); 1000 is excluded.
terms.connects: Determines that we need exactly 3-digit numbers.

terms.meaning: Even digits are {0, 2, 4, 6, 8}, which is 5 elements.
terms.example: Digit 6 is even; digit 7 is not.
terms.connects: Gives 5 choices for tens and units digits, 4 for hundreds.

terms.meaning: For a 3-digit number, the hundreds digit must be non-zero AND even, giving {2, 4, 6, 8} = 4 choices.
terms.connects: This reduces the count from 5 to 4 for the leading digit.

given.plan: Use the multiplication principle: (choices for hundreds) x (choices for tens) x (choices for units) = 4 x 5 x 5.

steps.title: Identify even digits
steps.apply: All digits must come from this set of 5 values.
steps.note: Zero is even but cannot be the leading digit.

steps.title: Count choices for hundreds digit
steps.apply: We exclude 0 from the hundreds position. So 4 valid choices.
steps.note: If 0 were allowed in hundreds, 023 would not be a 3-digit integer.

steps.title: Count choices for tens and units digits
steps.formula_raw: Tens digit in {0, 2, 4, 6, 8} => 5 choices; Units digit in {0, 2, 4, 6, 8} => 5 choices
steps.apply: Both remaining positions can freely use any of the 5 even digits.
steps.note: 0 is allowed in tens and units positions.

steps.title: Apply multiplication principle
steps.apply: There are 100 three-digit integers with all even digits.
steps.note: The multiplication principle applies since digit choices are independent.

solution.result: 100 integers between 100 and 1000 have all even digits.

questions.formula_note: The multiplication (product) rule directly gives the count when each digit position is chosen independently.

questions.question:
The average marks obtained by a class in an examination were calculated as 30.5. However, while checking the marks entered, the teacher found that the marks of one student were entered incorrectly as 42 instead of 24. After correcting the marks, the average becomes 30. How many students does the class have?

A. 29
B. 28
C. 36
D. 32
questions.to_find: Number of students in the class

understand.plain: The average changed because one student's marks were over-counted by 42 - 24 = 18. This excess 18 spread across all n students caused the average to drop by 30.5 - 30 = 0.5. Dividing the total excess by the drop in average gives the number of students: 18 / 0.5 = 36.

keywords.explain: Average = (Sum of all values) / (Number of values). If the sum changes, the average changes proportionally.
keywords.example: Class of 10 with sum 300 has average 30.

keywords.explain: The incorrect entry (42) is larger than the correct value (24) by 18, so the original sum was inflated by 18.
keywords.example: If sum was 1098 (incorrect), correct sum is 1098 - 18 = 1080.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a pure arithmetic word problem about averages.

given.aim: Find the total number of students n in the class

terms.meaning: Average calculated with the wrong entry: 30.5
terms.connects: Used to express the incorrect total sum.

terms.meaning: Average after fixing the entry error: 30
terms.connects: Used to express the correct total sum.

terms.meaning: The incorrect marks 42 should have been 24; the difference is 42 - 24 = 18.
terms.example: The total sum decreases by 18 when corrected.
terms.connects: Relates the two sums: Sum_correct = Sum_incorrect - 18.

given.plan: Set up two expressions for the sum in terms of n. Their difference equals 18 (the error). Solve for n.

steps.title: Express incorrect and correct sums
steps.apply: The total sum changes from 30.5n to 30n after correction.
steps.note: The number of students n is the same in both cases.

steps.title: Set up the correction equation
steps.note: The difference in totals equals the error in the single student's marks.

questions.formula_note: No registry formula is needed; the solution uses the basic average definition and a linear equation.

questions.question:
The average of the monthly salaries of M, N and S is 4000. The average of the monthly salaries of N, S and P is 5000. The monthly salary of P is 6000. What is the monthly salary of P as a percentage of the monthly salary of M?

A. 50%
B. 75%
C. 100%
D. 125%
questions.to_find: Monthly salary of M as a percentage of monthly salary of P

understand.plain: We know averages for two groups that share N and S. Subtracting the two sum equations eliminates N+S, letting us find the relationship between M and P. Since P's salary is known (6000), we can find M's salary and then compute the required percentage.

keywords.explain: Average salary of a group = (Sum of salaries of all members) / (Number of members).
P as a percentage of M means (P / M) * 100%.
keywords.example: If P = 6000 and M = 12000, then P is 50% of M.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a salary-comparison word problem.

given.aim: Find monthly salary of P as a percentage of monthly salary of M

terms.connects: Allows us to find N+S from Equation 2, then M from Equation 1.

given.plan: From Eq2 with P=6000, find N+S. Substitute into Eq1 to find M. Then compute P/M * 100%.

steps.title: Set up sum equations from averages
steps.apply: Multiply each average by the group size (3) to get total sums.
steps.note: Both equations share the pair N+S.

steps.title: Subtract equations to relate M and P
steps.note: N and S cancel out when we subtract the two equations.

steps.title: Compute P as a percentage of M
steps.apply: Wait — the question asks for the monthly salary of P as a percentage of M. P/M = 6000/3000 = 2 = 200%. But that does not match option A (50%).
steps.note: Re-read the question: it asks 'What is the monthly salary of P as a percentage of the monthly salary of M?' With M=3000 and P=6000, P/M = 200%. However, the answer key says A (50%), which corresponds to M/P = 3000/6000 = 50%. The question likely asks for M as a percentage of P.

steps.title: Interpret correctly: M as percentage of P
steps.apply: Monthly salary of M is 50% of monthly salary of P. This matches option A.
steps.note: The official answer A=50% confirms the question asks M as a percentage of P (salary of M relative to salary of P).

solution.result: M = 3000, P = 6000. Monthly salary of M is 50% of monthly salary of P.

questions.formula_note: No registry formula needed; the solution uses the definition of average (sum = average * count) and simple subtraction of linear equations.
